Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned AJ133 engine typically costs £5,500–£9,000 supply only, with used units available from £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,000 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Range Rover.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the year. Pre-2013 cars are high-risk — the timing chain tensioner design flaw is well-documented. Post-2013 cars received revised timing components and are significantly more robust. Both generations require diligent maintenance, particularly on the cooling system. With proper care, the AJ133 is a durable and rewarding engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner and guide wear (pre-2013), cooling system plastic component failures, supercharger coupler wear, and oil consumption. The timing chain is the single most critical issue — address it preventatively or risk a full engine replacement.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Range Rover values remain strong — a well-kept L405 is often worth £20,000–£50,000+. A £6,000–£9,000 reconditioned engine is only 15–30% of the vehicle's value — well within the Land Rover 60% scrap threshold. Replacement is almost always worthwhile on a cherished example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is AJ133 — it's the only 5.0-litre supercharged V8 used in the Range Rover. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself. Production years: L322 (2009-2012) and L405 (2013-2021).

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the AJ133 timing chain tensioner, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It needs replacement — the tensioner pins wear through the aluminium guides. A full timing chain, tensioner, guide and VVT sprocket replacement costs £2,500–£4,500. It's a labour-intensive job but far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£5,500+). The revised 2013+ components are the correct fix. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged market. This badge spans two generations — see the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing. Post-2013 engines typically command a premium.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged engine typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare 5.0 Supercharged engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner and Guide Wear (Pre-2013)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 1–5 seconds, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, misfire codes, eventual cam timing errors
problems.repairCost: £2,500–£4,500 (timing chain, tensioner, guide and VVT sprocket replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any cold-start rattle on a pre-2013 AJ133 requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: This is the single biggest risk on the 5.0 Supercharged. The original timing chain tensioner has a stainless steel pin that wears a hole through the aluminium chain guide. Once the guide is holed, the chain loses tension and can jump timing. The failure is so well-documented that there was a class action lawsuit. The fix is to replace the entire timing chain system with the revised 2013+ components. If you're buying a pre-2013 car and there's no proof this work has been done, budget for it immediately — or walk away. Frequent oil changes (every 5,000 miles) are the key to avoiding this issue.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Cooling System Plastic Component Failures
problems.symptoms: Gradual coolant loss, dampness around the engine bay, rising temperature gauge, steam from bonnet, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (coolant crossover pipes, thermostat housing, water pump)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating causes engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The AJ133's cooling system is full of plastic components that become brittle with age and heat cycles. The plastic thermostat housing and crossover pipes are known failure points. A cracked plastic pipe can dump coolant quickly, leading to rapid overheating and potential head gasket failure. Upgraded aluminium parts are available from specialist suppliers. If you own a 5.0 Supercharged, inspect the cooling system regularly and consider replacing plastic components preventatively at around 80,000 miles.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler and Snout Bearing Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling or grinding noise from the supercharger at idle, metallic tick that changes with engine speed, reduced power delivery
problems.recommendation: The supercharger coupler is a known wear item on higher‑mileage AJ133 engines. It can cause a rattling noise that owners often mistake for timing chain rattle. Replacement is a specialist job but well-understood by JLR performance specialists. Catch it early and it's a relatively inexpensive fix. Ignore it and the supercharger bearings can fail, requiring a full supercharger rebuild or replacement.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: The single most important upgrade — fit revised 2013+ components
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Straightforward replacement — fit upgraded aluminium parts
Simple replacement — well-understood by JLR specialists
Viable on a low‑mileage example; borderline on high‑mileage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged? Check These First
buyIf: * Post‑2013 build date — revised timing components make this a significantly safer buy
* Pre‑2013 with documented timing chain upgrade using 2013+ revised parts — this is the single most important check
* Full service history with frequent oil changes (every 5,000–8,000 miles recommended)
* Evidence of cooling system maintenance — water pump, thermostat, or crossover pipes replaced
avoidIf: * Pre‑2013 with no proof of timing chain work — you're buying a ticking time bomb
* Any cold‑start rattle — this is almost certainly timing chain tensioner wear
* Evidence of repeated coolant top‑ups without a diagnosed leak — plastic cooling system components are failing
* Poor service history — the AJ133 is unforgiving of neglected oil changes

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 5.0 Supercharged is a genuinely iconic engine — 510 bhp, supercharged V8 character, and the heart of the ultimate Range Rover. But it has known weaknesses. Pre‑2013 cars are high‑risk; the timing chain tensioner design flaw is well‑documented and expensive to fix. Post‑2013 cars are significantly improved but still require diligent maintenance, particularly on the cooling system. Buy on history, maintain religiously, and this engine will reward you with performance that few SUVs can match. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare 5.0 Supercharged engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover 5.0 Supercharged (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning) — this is the single most critical check on the AJ133
2. Service History — including which generation (L322 or L405) and evidence of timing chain work on pre‑2013 cars
3. Coolant Level — Any signs of leaks around the plastic crossover pipes or thermostat housing?
4. Supercharger Noise — Any rattling or grinding at idle? (coupler wear warning)
5. Oil Consumption — Many AJ133 engines burn 1L of oil per 1,000 miles — not fatal, but if ignored, low oil leads to spun bearings
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
7. Misfire History — Persistent misfires on cold start can indicate HPFP or injector issues
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (catalyst, O2 sensor issues)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — one engine code across two generations, with revised internal components from 2013.
